celebration: Two sounds, one team — the oo leaf goes up. The class learns that oo can hum long (moon, zoo) or clip short (book, look), and the heart word SCHOOL joins them: too important to wait for.

songs.title: Oo-Oo-Moon!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(oo… oo… oo…)
Two little o's!

[Hook]
Oo! Oo! Oo-oo-oo!
Two o's, ONE team!
Oo-oo-moon! Oo-oo-moon!
Look at the moon! (the moon!)

[Verse 2]
Oo! Oo! Oo-oo-oo!
Long oo — zoo!
Long oo — spoon!
Up on the roof! (the roof!)

[Verse 3]
Oo! Oo! Oo-oo-oo!
Short oo — book!
Short oo — look!
One team, two sounds! (oo! oo!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not oo!
(oo… oo… oo…)

[Outro]
Long moon… short book… (oo… oo…)
Look! (the moon!)
(oo… shhh…)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: oo has TWO sounds — the long moon /uː/ (a low owl-hoot) and the short book /ʊ/ (a quick pull). Sing them contrasted: verse 2 holds the long, verse 3 clips the short. 'oo' sung as the pure vowel, never letter names 'oh-oh'. The whole lesson: one team, two jobs. Potato bridge intact.

songs.title: Look at the Moon
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(We can read. We can read!)

[Hook]
Look at the moon!
Look at the moon!
Where is the moon? (where? where?)
Up on the roof!

[Verse 2]
Star is on the roof.
Star can see the moon.
Cool is the moon! (cool! cool!)
Look, Star, look!

[Verse 3]
Sheep is on the roof too!
The moon is at the school.
Look at the moon and the school!
(soon… soon…)

[Outro]
The roof… the moon… (shhh…)
What is on the roof?!
(POTATO!)
Look at the moon. (goodnight, moon.)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: Frame: 'Look at the moon.' — a quiet rooftop night, Star and Sheep moon-gazing. Introduces the heart word SCHOOL (sung whole, taught by heart — sch is irregular). Keep it hushed and lullaby-slow under the trap; end on 'goodnight, moon' after the potato gag.

spreads.artNote: A huge pale moon low over a dark rooftop, spotlight-bright. The whole book glows silver.
The cool blue-white moon filling the sky, its craters just visible. Serene.
Star — the warm golden five-point star with a serene sleepy face — perched on the ridge of a rooftop, looking up.
Star gazing up in wonder at the enormous moon, its gold glow answering the moon's silver.

spreads.text: Sheep is on the roof too.
spreads.artNote: Sheep clambering up beside Star on the rooftop, both moon-gazing. 'Too' — the long oo again.

spreads.text: The moon is big and cool.
spreads.artNote: Wide shot: Star and Sheep small on the rooftop, the moon vast above them. Quiet spread — read it slow.

spreads.text: Look! The moon is at the school.
spreads.artNote: The moon now hangs right over a little schoolhouse down the lane. The heart word SCHOOL, printed and pointed to.

spreads.text: Star and Sheep look at the moon.
spreads.artNote: Star and Sheep side by side on the ridge, silhouetted, gazing at the school and the moon together. Peace.
A single potato has appeared on the rooftop between Star and Sheep, wearing tiny round moon-gazing glasses. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it. Star's sleepy eyes open a little wider.

extras: TWO-SOUND SORT: two hoops — long oo (moon, zoo, spoon, broom, food, tooth) and short oo (book, look, cook, foot, wood, good). The child sorts by EAR. The lesson is that a spelling can hold two sounds — the first real 'reader's choice' of decoding, and a gentle bridge to the ambiguity of English.
HEART WORD school: sch is not decodable by rule (it says /sk/), so school joins the heart-word wall today. Too central to a child's life to hold back — they read it, they don't sound it out.
Pattern Tree: the class reads moon/zoo/book/look aloud and hangs the oo leaf.
Moveable alphabet: build m-oo-n, z-oo, b-oo-k, l-oo-k, r-oo-f, sp-oo-n — noticing the same oo hums differently in moon and book.

teacherFocus: oo is the first team with TWO jobs: the long moon /uː/ and the short book /ʊ/. Don't teach a rule for which — there isn't a reliable one; teach the EAR. Two hoops, sort by sound, and let the child feel the mouth pull long for moon and clip short for book. This is the gentle on-ramp to English's ambiguity, right after the clean single-sound teams. Add the heart word SCHOOL to the wall today — sch says /sk/, no rule covers it, and the word is too central to a child's world to withhold. Hang the oo leaf.
